video_title: Priyamvada Natarajan: The Mystery of Dark Matter
gist: Yale astrophysicist Priyamvada Natarajan walks through the dark-matter problem (data, simulation, theory, prediction), arguing new multiscale cluster-lensing data breaks the standard cold-collisionless dark-matter model on small scales and may require a self-interacting or two-particle dark sector. A pure-science interview with essentially no market or investment content.

insights.claim: A Yale cosmologist describes astrophysics/basic-science as competitive and underfunded, with recent government budget cuts she frames as a loss of perspective on the importance of basic science.
insights.speaker: guest, Priyamvada Natarajan (Yale astrophysicist)
insights.so_what: Soft macro signal only: continued tightening of US federal basic-research budgets is a mild headwind for science-adjacent grant-funded sectors; not directly actionable.
insights.quote: the reality is it's a competitive field which is not sadly um not as well funded as it should be. And right now with the complete kind of loss of perspective of how important basic sciences is and all the cuts

insights.claim: The decades-old debate over whether numerical simulations merely ratify inputs or can generate genuine discoveries directly mirrors the current debate over whether AI will only accelerate human discovery or generate insights humans could not reach alone.
insights.speaker: guest, Priyamvada Natarajan (Yale astrophysicist)
insights.so_what: Conceptual framing only: an expert who has lived the simulation-generativity debate leans toward simulations (and by analogy AI) being genuinely generative, an optimistic prior on AI-for-science productivity but no tradeable specifics.
